Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Switcher locomotive"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")), **shunter locomotive** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")), **station pilot** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")), or **shifter locomotive** ([Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") terminology) is a [locomotive](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") used for maneuvering railway vehicles over short distances. Switchers do not usually move [trains](/wiki/Train "Train") over long distances. Instead, they typically assemble trains in order for another locomotive to take over. Switchers often operate in a [railyard](/wiki/Railyard "Railyard") or make short transfer runs. They may serve as the primary motive power on short [branch lines](/wiki/Branch_line "Branch line") or [switching and terminal railroads](/wiki/Switching_and_terminal_railroad "Switching and terminal railroad").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) A hybrid type known as a [road switcher](/wiki/Road_switcher "Road switcher") can both shunt and haul trains.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Shunter (rail)](/w/index.php?title=Shunter_(rail)&redirect=no "Shunter (rail)"))

Locomotive used in yards for assembling trains

"Switcher" redirects here. For other uses, see [Switcher (disambiguation)](/wiki/Switcher_(disambiguation) "Switcher (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Switcher_locomotive "Special:EditPage/Switcher locomotive") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Switcher locomotive"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Switcher+locomotive%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Switcher+locomotive%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Lokomotiva_742.738_v_CZ_LOKO_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lokomotiva_742.738_v_CZ_LOKO_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lokomotiva_742.738_v_CZ_LOKO_(cropped).jpg)

[CZ Loko Effishunter](/wiki/CZ_Loko "CZ Loko")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/SBB_Ee_922_021_in_Brig_VS.jpg/250px-SBB_Ee_922_021_in_Brig_VS.jpg)](/wiki/File:SBB_Ee_922_021_in_Brig_VS.jpg)

[Stadler Rail Ee 922](/wiki/SBB-CFF-FFS_Ee_922 "SBB-CFF-FFS Ee 922")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/03066%2C_Barrow_Hill_Roundhouse..JPG/250px-03066%2C_Barrow_Hill_Roundhouse..JPG)](/wiki/File:03066,_Barrow_Hill_Roundhouse..JPG)

[British Rail Class 03](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_03 "British Rail Class 03")

A **switcher locomotive** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")), **shunter locomotive** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")), **station pilot** ([British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")), or **shifter locomotive** ([Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") terminology) is a [locomotive](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") used for maneuvering railway vehicles over short distances. Switchers do not usually move [trains](/wiki/Train "Train") over long distances. Instead, they typically assemble trains in order for another locomotive to take over. Switchers often operate in a [railyard](/wiki/Railyard "Railyard") or make short transfer runs. They may serve as the primary motive power on short [branch lines](/wiki/Branch_line "Branch line") or [switching and terminal railroads](/wiki/Switching_and_terminal_railroad "Switching and terminal railroad").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) A hybrid type known as a [road switcher](/wiki/Road_switcher "Road switcher") can both shunt and haul trains.

Switchers are optimized for their role, being relatively low-powered but with a high starting [tractive effort](/wiki/Tractive_effort "Tractive effort") for getting heavy cars rolling quickly. Switchers are geared to produce high [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") but are restricted to low top speeds and have small diameter [driving wheels](/wiki/Driving_wheel "Driving wheel"). Switchers tend to be durable and to remain in service for a long time,[[4]](#cite_note-4) such as the [Swedish class U](/wiki/SJ_U "SJ U").

American, Russian, Indian and Chinese switchers tend to be larger, with [bogies](/wiki/Bogie "Bogie") to allow them to be used on tight radiuses. Western European shunters tend to be smaller and more often have fixed axles[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. They also often maintained [coupling rods](/wiki/Coupling_rods "Coupling rods") for longer than other locomotive types, although bogie types have long been used where very heavy loads are involved, such as at steelworks.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

A switcher may also be called a yard pilot, switch engine, or yard goat.

The term can also be used to describe the workers operating these engines or engaged in directing shunting operations. Switching locomotives may be purpose-built engines, but may also be downgraded main-line engines, or simply main-line engines assigned to switching. Switchers can also be used on short excursion train rides.

## Power types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Power types")]

### Diesel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Diesel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Shinano_Railway_Sakaki_Station_freight_car_switchingFreight_train_switching_%2846633855415%29.jpg/250px-Shinano_Railway_Sakaki_Station_freight_car_switchingFreight_train_switching_%2846633855415%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shinano_Railway_Sakaki_Station_freight_car_switchingFreight_train_switching_(46633855415).jpg)

A diesel switcher in [Sakaki](/wiki/Sakaki,_Nagano "Sakaki, Nagano"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan")

[Diesel](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") switchers tend to have a high cab and often lower and/or narrower [hoods](/wiki/Hood_(vehicle) "Hood (vehicle)") (bonnets) containing the diesel engines, for all round visibility. [Slugs](/wiki/Slug_(railroad) "Slug (railroad)") are often used because they allow even greater tractive effort to be applied. Nearly all slugs used for switching are of the low hood, cabless variety. Good visibility in both directions is critical, because a switcher may be running in either direction; turning the locomotive is time-consuming. Some earlier diesel switchers used [cow–calf](/wiki/Cow%E2%80%93calf "Cow–calf") configurations of two powered units in order to provide greater power.

Modern diesel switchers are usually [diesel-electric locomotives](/wiki/Diesel%E2%80%93electric_locomotive "Diesel–electric locomotive").

### Electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Electric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/1163003_at_Salzburg_Hauptbahnhof.jpg/250px-1163003_at_Salzburg_Hauptbahnhof.jpg)](/wiki/File:1163003_at_Salzburg_Hauptbahnhof.jpg)

An electric switcher in [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria")

The majority of modern switchers are diesels, but countries with near-total [electrification](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification"), like Switzerland, use [electric](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive") switchers. Prior to the introduction of diesel-electric locomotives, electric shunting locomotives were used to an extent in Great Britain where heavy trains needed to be started on steep gradients. The steeply-graded Quayside Branch in [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") was electrified by the [North Eastern Railway](/wiki/North_Eastern_Railway_(UK) "North Eastern Railway (UK)") in 1905, and two steeplecab locomotives were built to handle all traffic on the line. One of these, [No. 1](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_ES1 "British Rail Class ES1"), is now part of the National Collection and resides at [Locomotion](/wiki/Shildon_Locomotion_Museum "Shildon Locomotion Museum") in [Shildon](/wiki/Shildon "Shildon"). On the opposite side of the Tyne, the electrified lines owned by the Harton Coal Company in [South Shields](/wiki/South_Shields "South Shields") for the movement of coal and colliery waste to shipping facilities on the river was one of the more extensive industrial networks. A number of the early [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") locomotives built for use on these lines have been preserved.

Electric locomotives were also extensively employed for moving the coke cars at [cokeworks](/wiki/Coke_(fuel) "Coke (fuel)"), obtaining power from a side wire, as [third rail](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") or [overhead line](/wiki/Pantograph_(rail) "Pantograph (rail)") electrification would have been impractical. These specialised locomotives were tall steeple-cab types not seen anywhere else, and operated on a short length of track between the ovens and the quenching tower. Despite their ubiquity, very few have survived into preservation as there is very little scope of operating them due to their unique means of obtaining power, slow speed and the fact they greatly exceed the loading gauge of most railway lines. One example built by [Greenwood and Batley](/wiki/Greenwood_and_Batley "Greenwood and Batley") in [Armley](/wiki/Armley "Armley"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") is preserved at the [Middleton Railway](/wiki/Middleton_Railway "Middleton Railway"), not far from where it was built.

Small industrial shunters are sometimes battery powered type. An early battery-powered shunting locomotive is shown here.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [Tyne and Wear Metro](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear_Metro "Tyne and Wear Metro") has three battery electric shunters built by [Hunslet](/wiki/Hunslet_Engine_Company "Hunslet Engine Company"), which are used to haul engineering trains when the overhead supply is switched off. [New Zealand Railways](/wiki/New_Zealand_Railways_Department "New Zealand Railways Department") imported and manufactured locally battery-electric shunters in the 1920s: the [EB class](/wiki/New_Zealand_EB_class_locomotive "New Zealand EB class locomotive") and the [E class (1922)](/wiki/New_Zealand_E_class_locomotive_(1922) "New Zealand E class locomotive (1922)")

### Electro-diesel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Electro-diesel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Tem_346.jpg/250px-Tem_346.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tem_346.jpg)

A small [electro-diesel](/wiki/Electro-diesel_locomotive "Electro-diesel locomotive") switcher in [Pfäffikon](/wiki/Pf%C3%A4ffikon,_Schwyz "Pfäffikon, Schwyz"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland")

Some switchers are [electro-diesel](/wiki/Electro-diesel_locomotive "Electro-diesel locomotive"), and hence can be powered from onboard diesel engines, or from an external electricity supply.

### Steam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Steam")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/National_Railway_Museum_%288711%29.jpg/250px-National_Railway_Museum_%288711%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Railway_Museum_(8711).jpg)

A typical British steam shunter, a [GNR Class J13](/wiki/GNR_Class_J13 "GNR Class J13")

Steam shunter/switchers are now mostly out of service. [Steam](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive") switchers were either [tank locomotives](/wiki/Tank_locomotive "Tank locomotive") or had special (smaller) [tenders](/wiki/Tender_(locomotive) "Tender (locomotive)"), with narrow [coal bunkers](/wiki/Coal_bunker "Coal bunker") and/or sloped tender decks to increase rearward visibility. Headlights, where carried, were mounted on both ends. Most were either side-tank or saddle-tank types, however in the usual departure from its neighbours' practice, the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") used [pannier](/wiki/Pannier_tank "Pannier tank") tanks for shunting and branch line work, a practice which the Western Region of BR perpetuated until steam traction was phased out, with several examples joining a [9F](/wiki/BR_standard_class_9F "BR standard class 9F") as banking engines to assist locomotives on the notoriously arduous ascent of the Lickey Incline, replacing the [LMS "Jinties"](/wiki/LMS_Fowler_Class_3F "LMS Fowler Class 3F") which had formerly carried out the task alongside ["Big Bertha"](/wiki/MR_0-10-0_Lickey_Banker "MR 0-10-0 Lickey Banker").

As diesel shunters began to appear in ever-increasing numbers, attempts were made by companies such as Sentinel to adapt the vertical boilers from their steam powered road vehicles for use in shunting locomotives, in order to compete with the newcomers. Although these were found to be equal in power and efficiency to most of the early diesel designs, their development came too late to have any real impact. Outwardly, they bear more resemblance to diesels than steam locomotives. A number have been preserved on [heritage railways](/wiki/Heritage_railway "Heritage railway"), although few of these are in working order, being designed very specifically for shunting work and lacking the necessary speed to travel any kind of distance.

Small industrial shunters have sometimes been [fireless locomotives](/wiki/Fireless_locomotive "Fireless locomotive") and a few of these are still at work in Germany. Again, several have been preserved, but are mostly static displays, as heritage railways and museums lack the large source of high-pressure steam (such as a [power station](/wiki/Power_station "Power station")'s boilers) needed to charge the locomotive's accumulator.

## By region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: By region")]

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/San_Diego_Imperial_Valley_switcher_702_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-San_Diego_Imperial_Valley_switcher_702_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Diego_Imperial_Valley_switcher_702_(cropped).jpg)

A modern  [NRE Genset](/wiki/NRE_3GS21B "NRE 3GS21B") switcher on the American [San Diego and Imperial Valley Railroad](/wiki/San_Diego_and_Imperial_Valley_Railroad "San Diego and Imperial Valley Railroad")

[American](/wiki/United_States "United States") switchers tend to be larger, and are almost always powered by diesel.

Most American switchers are actually [road switchers](/wiki/Road_switcher_locomotive "Road switcher locomotive"), which are larger and have greater power output, to be used on mainlines.

### Great Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Great Britain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Old_Oak_Common_-_GWR_08836_running_up_through_the_yard.JPG/250px-Old_Oak_Common_-_GWR_08836_running_up_through_the_yard.JPG)](/wiki/File:Old_Oak_Common_-_GWR_08836_running_up_through_the_yard.JPG)

The [British Rail Class 08](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_08 "British Rail Class 08") is a widely used shunter in Great Britain

British shunters are much smaller than those used in the United States. Current British shunters are [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0") diesel-electrics, [Class 08](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_08 "British Rail Class 08") and [Class 09](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_09 "British Rail Class 09"), of 350-400 horsepower. These were developed from similar locomotives supplied by the [English Electric](/wiki/English_Electric "English Electric") Company to the [Big Four British railway companies](/wiki/Big_Four_British_railway_companies "Big Four British railway companies") in the 1930s and 1940s, e.g. those pioneered by the [LMS](/wiki/LMS_diesel_shunters "LMS diesel shunters"). Similar locomotives were exported to the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") (e.g. [NS Class 600](/wiki/NS_Class_600 "NS Class 600")) and Australia (e.g. [Victorian Railways F class (diesel)](/wiki/Victorian_Railways_F_class_(diesel) "Victorian Railways F class (diesel)")). The use of shunting locomotives saw a sharp decline in Britain in the latter half of the 20th century, largely due to the contraction of the network, increased competition from road traffic and widespread adoption of train-load freight, with fixed rakes of wagons moving mainly bulk products between rapid-loading facilities, as opposed to thousands of sidings and goods depots feeding trains of assorted wagons into the marshalling yards.

### Continental Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Switcher_locomotive&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Continental Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/TCDD_DH33121.jpg/250px-TCDD_DH33121.jpg)](/wiki/File:TCDD_DH33121.jpg)

A [TCDD DH33100](/wiki/TCDD_DH33100 "TCDD DH33100") shunter in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")

In continental Europe [0-6-0](/wiki/0-6-0 "0-6-0") (or "C") diesel-hydraulics, similar to the short-lived [British Rail Class 14](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_14 "British Rail Class 14"), are widely used. A very common type is the [DB Class V 60](/wiki/DB_Class_V_60 "DB Class V 60") and its variants. For lightweight shunting of single wagons or short trains, two-axle shunters are common; in Germany these are known as [Kleinlokomotive](/wiki/Kleinlokomotive "Kleinlokomotive") (small locomotive).
